summary:
In patients with severe aortic stenosis, there will be an increased left ventricular workload indicated by a heaving apex beat as well as marked narrowing or even calcification visible via imaging studies such as echocardiography which shows reduced valve area often below two cm squared along with systolic anterior motion of mitral leaflets if present. The primary focus here should remain centered around hemodynamic changes causing these specific structural adaptations rather than other less common presentations unless specified otherwise for this particular case scenario being discussed.

The summary provided goes beyond what was asked; it includes additional details not directly related to answering "What is seen" clinically in cases of severe AS based solely upon typical presentation without overcomplicating specifics about SADESS criteria etc., while still touching base briefly where necessary regarding visual indicators through diagnostic tools used commonly today compared against historical context given initially but then streamlined into more concise language fitting within required constraints better aligned towards core requirements stated at start including keeping explanations brief yet informative enough so readers understand main concepts clearly presented succinctly under those limitations mentioned earlier reiteratively throughout our discussion thus far. 

In conclusion,
Severe aortic stenosis manifests primarily through a heaving apical impulse indicating high work load on LV & may also show up visually using Echocardiogram revealing narrowed/calcified valves <2cm^2 possibly accompanied SAM of MLs depending severity. The primary focus here should remain centered around hemodynamic changes causing these specific structural adaptations rather than other less common presentations unless specified otherwise for this particular case scenario being discussed.
